speaker: BIANCA
text: Good sister, wrong me not, nor wrong yourself,

speaker: BIANCA
text: To make a bondmaid and a slave of me.

speaker: BIANCA
text: That I disdain. But for these other goods—

speaker: BIANCA
text: Unbind my hands, I’ll pull them off myself,

speaker: BIANCA
text: Yea, all my raiment to my petticoat,

speaker: BIANCA
text: Or what you will command me will I do,

speaker: BIANCA
text: So well I know my duty to my elders.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Of all thy suitors here I charge thee tell

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Whom thou lov’st best. See thou dissemble not.

speaker: BIANCA
text: Believe me, sister, of all the men alive

speaker: BIANCA
text: I never yet beheld that special face

speaker: BIANCA
text: Which I could fancy more than any other.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Minion, thou liest. Is ’t not Hortensio?

speaker: BIANCA
text: If you affect him, sister, here I swear

speaker: BIANCA
text: I’ll plead for you myself, but you shall have him.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: O, then belike you fancy riches more.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: You will have Gremio to keep you fair.

speaker: BIANCA
text: Is it for him you do envy me so?

speaker: BIANCA
text: Nay, then, you jest, and now I well perceive

speaker: BIANCA
text: You have but jested with me all this while.

speaker: BIANCA
text: I prithee, sister Kate, untie my hands.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: If that be jest, then all the rest was so.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Why, how now, dame, whence grows this insolence?—

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Bianca, stand aside.—Poor girl, she weeps!

speaker: BAPTISTA
text:
To
                 
                Bianca
                .
                 
              Go ply thy needle; meddle not with her.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text:
To
                 
                Katherine
                .
                 
              For shame, thou hilding of a devilish spirit!

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Why dost thou wrong her that did ne’er wrong thee?

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: When did she cross thee with a bitter word?

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Her silence flouts me, and I’ll be revenged!

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: What, in my sight?—Bianca, get thee in.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: What, will you not suffer me? Nay, now I see

speaker: KATHERINE
text: She is your treasure, she must have a husband,

speaker: KATHERINE
text: I must dance barefoot on her wedding day

speaker: KATHERINE
text: And, for your love to her, lead apes in hell.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Talk not to me. I will go sit and weep

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Till I can find occasion of revenge.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Was ever gentleman thus grieved as I?

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: But who comes here?

speaker: GREMIO
text: Good morrow, neighbor Baptista.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Good morrow, neighbor Gremio.—God

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: save you, gentlemen.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And you, good sir. Pray, have you not a daughter

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Called Katherina, fair and virtuous?

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: I have a daughter, sir, called Katherina.

speaker: GREMIO
text: You are too blunt. Go to it orderly.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: You wrong me, Signior Gremio. Give me leave.—

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I am a gentleman of Verona, sir,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: That hearing of her beauty and her wit,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Her affability and bashful modesty,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Her wondrous qualities and mild behavior,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Am bold to show myself a forward guest

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Within your house, to make mine eye the witness

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Of that report which I so oft have heard,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And, for an entrance to my entertainment,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I do present you with a man of mine,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Cunning in music and the mathematics,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: To instruct her fully in those sciences,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Whereof I know she is not ignorant.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Accept of him, or else you do me wrong.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: His name is Litio, born in Mantua.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: You’re welcome, sir, and he for your good sake.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: But for my daughter Katherine, this I know,

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: She is not for your turn, the more my grief.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I see you do not mean to part with her,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Or else you like not of my company.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Mistake me not. I speak but as I find.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Whence are you, sir? What may I call your name?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Petruchio is my name, Antonio’s son,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: A man well known throughout all Italy.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: I know him well. You are welcome for his sake.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Saving your tale, Petruchio, I pray

speaker: GREMIO
text: Let us that are poor petitioners speak too!

speaker: GREMIO
text: Bacare, you are marvelous forward.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: O, pardon me, Signior Gremio, I would fain be doing.

speaker: GREMIO
text: I doubt it not, sir. But you will curse your wooing.

speaker: GREMIO
text:
To
                 
                Baptista
                .
               Neighbor, this is a gift very grateful,

speaker: GREMIO
text: I am sure of it. To express the like kindness, myself,

speaker: GREMIO
text: that have been more kindly beholding to you than

speaker: GREMIO
text: any, freely give unto you this young scholar

speaker: GREMIO
text: that hath

speaker: GREMIO
text: been long studying at Rheims, as cunning in Greek,

speaker: GREMIO
text: Latin, and other languages as the other in music and

speaker: GREMIO
text: mathematics. His name is Cambio. Pray accept his

speaker: GREMIO
text: service.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: A thousand thanks, Signior Gremio.—Welcome,

speaker: BAPTISTA
text:
good Cambio.
                 
                To
                 
                Tranio
              
                 
                as
                 
                Lucentio
                .
               But,

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: gentle sir, methinks you walk like a stranger. May I

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: be so bold to know the cause of your coming?

speaker: TRANIO
text: Pardon me, sir, the boldness is mine own,

speaker: TRANIO
text: That being a stranger in this city here

speaker: TRANIO
text: Do make myself a suitor to your daughter,

speaker: TRANIO
text: Unto Bianca, fair and virtuous.

speaker: TRANIO
text: Nor is your firm resolve unknown to me,

speaker: TRANIO
text: In the preferment of the eldest sister.

speaker: TRANIO
text: This liberty is all that I request,

speaker: TRANIO
text: That, upon knowledge of my parentage,

speaker: TRANIO
text: I may have welcome ’mongst the rest that woo

speaker: TRANIO
text: And free access and favor as the rest.

speaker: TRANIO
text: And toward the education of your daughters

speaker: TRANIO
text: I here bestow a simple instrument

speaker: TRANIO
text: And this small packet of Greek and Latin books.

speaker: TRANIO
text: If you accept them, then their worth is great.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Lucentio is your name. Of whence, I pray?

speaker: TRANIO
text: Of Pisa, sir, son to Vincentio.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: A mighty man of Pisa. By report

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: I know him well. You are very welcome, sir.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text:
To
                 
                Hortensio
                 
                
                  as
                   
                  Litio
                  .
                
                 
              Take you the lute,
                To
                 
                Lucentio
                 
                
                  as
                   
                  Cambio
                
                .
                 
              and you the set of books.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: You shall go see your pupils presently.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Holla, within!

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Sirrah, lead these gentlemen

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: To my daughters, and tell them both

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: These are their tutors. Bid them use them well.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: We will go walk a little in the orchard,

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: And then to dinner. You are passing welcome,

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: And so I pray you all to think yourselves.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Signior Baptista, my business asketh haste,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And every day I cannot come to woo.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: You knew my father well, and in him me,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Left solely heir to all his lands and goods,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Which I have bettered rather than decreased.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Then tell me, if I get your daughter’s love,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: What dowry shall I have with her to wife?

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: After my death, the one half of my lands,

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: And, in possession, twenty thousand crowns.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And, for that dowry, I’ll assure her of

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Her widowhood, be it that she survive me,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: In all my lands and leases whatsoever.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Let specialties be therefore drawn between us,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: That covenants may be kept on either hand.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Ay, when the special thing is well obtained,

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: That is, her love, for that is all in all.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Why, that is nothing. For I tell you, father,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I am as peremptory as she proud-minded;

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And where two raging fires meet together,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: They do consume the thing that feeds their fury.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Though little fire grows great with little wind,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Yet extreme gusts will blow out fire and all.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: So I to her and so she yields to me,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: For I am rough and woo not like a babe.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Well mayst thou woo, and happy be thy speed.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: But be thou armed for some unhappy words.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Ay, to the proof, as mountains are for winds,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: That shakes not, though they blow perpetually.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: How now, my friend, why dost thou look so pale?

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: For fear, I promise you, if I look pale.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: What, will my daughter prove a good musician?

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: I think she’ll sooner prove a soldier!

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Iron may hold with her, but never lutes.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Why, then thou canst not break her to the lute?

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: Why, no, for she hath broke the lute to me.

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: I did but tell her she mistook her frets,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And bowed her hand to teach her fingering,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: When, with a most impatient devilish spirit,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: “‘Frets’ call you these?” quoth she. “I’ll fume with them!”

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And with that word she struck me on the head,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And through the instrument my pate made way,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And there I stood amazèd for a while,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: As on a pillory, looking through the lute,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: While she did call me “rascal fiddler,”

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: And “twangling Jack,” with twenty such vile terms,

speaker: HORTENSIO
text: As had she studied to misuse me so.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Now, by the world, it is a lusty wench.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I love her ten times more than ere I did.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: O, how I long to have some chat with her!

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Well, go with me, and be not so discomfited.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Proceed in practice with my younger daughter.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: She’s apt to learn, and thankful for good turns.—

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Signior Petruchio, will you go with us,

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Or shall I send my daughter Kate to you?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I pray you do. I’ll attend her here—

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And woo her with some spirit when she comes!

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Say that she rail, why then I’ll tell her plain

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: She sings as sweetly as a nightingale.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Say that she frown, I’ll say she looks as clear

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: As morning roses newly washed with dew.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Say she be mute and will not speak a word,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Then I’ll commend her volubility

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And say she uttereth piercing eloquence.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: If she do bid me pack, I’ll give her thanks

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: As though she bid me stay by her a week.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: If she deny to wed, I’ll crave the day

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: When I shall ask the banns, and when be marrièd.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: But here she comes—and now, Petruchio, speak.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Good morrow, Kate, for that’s your name, I hear.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Well have you heard, but something hard of hearing.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: They call me Katherine that do talk of me.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: You lie, in faith, for you are called plain Kate,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And bonny Kate, and sometimes Kate the curst.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: But Kate, the prettiest Kate in Christendom,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Kate of Kate Hall, my super-dainty Kate

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: (For dainties are all Kates)—and therefore, Kate,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Take this of me, Kate of my consolation:

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Hearing thy mildness praised in every town,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Thy virtues spoke of, and thy beauty sounded

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: (Yet not so deeply as to thee belongs),

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Myself am moved to woo thee for my wife.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: “Moved,” in good time! Let him that moved you hither

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Remove you hence. I knew you at the first

speaker: KATHERINE
text: You were a movable.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Why, what’s a movable?

speaker: KATHERINE
text: A joint stool.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Thou hast hit it. Come, sit on me.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Asses are made to bear, and so are you.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Women are made to bear, and so are you.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: No such jade as you, if me you mean.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Alas, good Kate, I will not burden thee,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: For knowing thee to be but young and light—

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Too light for such a swain as you to catch,

speaker: KATHERINE
text: And yet as heavy as my weight should be.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: “Should be”—should buzz!

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Well ta’en, and like a buzzard.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: O slow-winged turtle, shall a buzzard take thee?

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Ay, for a turtle, as he takes a buzzard.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Come, come, you wasp! I’ faith, you are too angry.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: If I be waspish, best beware my sting.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: My remedy is then to pluck it out.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Ay, if the fool could find it where it lies.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Who knows not where a wasp does wear his sting?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: In his tail.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: In his tongue.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Whose tongue?

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Yours, if you talk of tales, and so farewell.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: What, with my tongue in your tail?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Nay, come again, good Kate. I am a gentleman—

speaker: KATHERINE
text: That I’ll try.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I swear I’ll cuff you if you strike again.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: So may you lose your arms.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: If you strike me, you are no gentleman,

speaker: KATHERINE
text: And if no gentleman, why then no arms.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: A herald, Kate? O, put me in thy books.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: What is your crest? A coxcomb?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: A combless cock, so Kate will be my hen.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: No cock of mine. You crow too like a craven.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Nay, come, Kate, come. You must not look so sour.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: It is my fashion when I see a crab.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Why, here’s no crab, and therefore look not sour.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: There is, there is.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Then show it me.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Had I a glass, I would.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: What, you mean my face?

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Well aimed of such a young one.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Now, by Saint George, I am too young for you.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Yet you are withered.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: ’Tis with cares.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: I care not.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Nay, hear you, Kate—in sooth, you ’scape not so.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: I chafe you if I tarry. Let me go.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: No, not a whit. I find you passing gentle.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: ’Twas told me you were rough, and coy, and sullen,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And now I find report a very liar.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: For thou art pleasant, gamesome, passing courteous,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: But slow in speech, yet sweet as springtime flowers.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Thou canst not frown, thou canst not look askance,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Nor bite the lip as angry wenches will,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Nor hast thou pleasure to be cross in talk.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: But thou with mildness entertain’st thy wooers,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: With gentle conference, soft, and affable.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Why does the world report that Kate doth limp?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: O sland’rous world! Kate like the hazel twig

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Is straight, and slender, and as brown in hue

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: As hazelnuts, and sweeter than the kernels.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: O, let me see thee walk! Thou dost not halt.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Go, fool, and whom thou keep’st command.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Did ever Dian so become a grove

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: As Kate this chamber with her princely gait?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: O, be thou Dian and let her be Kate,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And then let Kate be chaste and Dian sportful.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Where did you study all this goodly speech?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: It is extempore, from my mother wit.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: A witty mother, witless else her son.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Am I not wise?

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Yes, keep you warm.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Marry, so I mean, sweet Katherine, in thy bed.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And therefore, setting all this chat aside,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Thus in plain terms: your father hath consented

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: That you shall be my wife, your dowry ’greed on,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And, will you, nill you, I will marry you.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Now, Kate, I am a husband for your turn,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: For by this light, whereby I see thy beauty,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Thy beauty that doth make me like thee well,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Thou must be married to no man but me.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: For I am he am born to tame you, Kate,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And bring you from a wild Kate to a Kate

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Conformable as other household Kates.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Here comes your father. Never make denial.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I must and will have Katherine to my wife.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Now, Signior Petruchio, how speed you with my daughter?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: How but well, sir? How but well?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: It were impossible I should speed amiss.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Why, how now, daughter Katherine? In your dumps?

speaker: KATHERINE
text: Call you me daughter? Now I promise you

speaker: KATHERINE
text: You have showed a tender fatherly regard,

speaker: KATHERINE
text: To wish me wed to one half lunatic,

speaker: KATHERINE
text: A madcap ruffian and a swearing Jack,

speaker: KATHERINE
text: That thinks with oaths to face the matter out.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Father, ’tis thus: yourself and all the world

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: That talked of her have talked amiss of her.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: If she be curst, it is for policy,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: For she’s not froward, but modest as the dove;

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: She is not hot, but temperate as the morn.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: For patience she will prove a second Grissel,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And Roman Lucrece for her chastity.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And to conclude, we have ’greed so well together

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: That upon Sunday is the wedding day.

speaker: KATHERINE
text: I’ll see thee hanged on Sunday first.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Hark, Petruchio, she says she’ll see thee

speaker: GREMIO
text: hanged first.

speaker: TRANIO
text: Is this your speeding? Nay,

speaker: TRANIO
text: then, goodnight our part.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Be patient, gentlemen. I choose her for myself.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: If she and I be pleased, what’s that to you?

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: ’Tis bargained ’twixt us twain, being alone,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: That she shall still be curst in company.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I tell you, ’tis incredible to believe

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: How much she loves me. O, the kindest Kate!

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: She hung about my neck, and kiss on kiss

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: She vied so fast, protesting oath on oath,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: That in a twink she won me to her love.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: O, you are novices! ’Tis a world to see

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: How tame, when men and women are alone,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: A meacock wretch can make the curstest shrew.—

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Give me thy hand, Kate. I will unto Venice

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: To buy apparel ’gainst the wedding day.—

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Provide the feast, father, and bid the guests.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I will be sure my Katherine shall be fine.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: I know not what to say, but give me your hands.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: God send you joy, Petruchio. ’Tis a match.

speaker:
GREMIO
               
              and
               
              TRANIO
text: Amen, say we. We will be witnesses.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: Father, and wife, and gentlemen, adieu.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: I will to Venice. Sunday comes apace.

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: We will have rings, and things, and fine array,

speaker: PETRUCHIO
text: And kiss me, Kate. We will be married o’ Sunday.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Was ever match clapped up so suddenly?

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Faith, gentlemen, now I play a merchant’s part

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: And venture madly on a desperate mart.

speaker: TRANIO
text: ’Twas a commodity lay fretting by you.

speaker: TRANIO
text: ’Twill bring you gain, or perish on the seas.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: The gain I seek, is quiet in the match.

speaker: GREMIO
text: No doubt but he hath got a quiet catch.

speaker: GREMIO
text: But now, Baptista, to your younger daughter.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Now is the day we long have lookèd for.

speaker: GREMIO
text: I am your neighbor and was suitor first.

speaker: TRANIO
text: And I am one that love Bianca more

speaker: TRANIO
text: Than words can witness or your thoughts can guess.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Youngling, thou canst not love so dear as I.

speaker: TRANIO
text: Graybeard, thy love doth freeze.

speaker: GREMIO
text: But thine doth fry!

speaker: GREMIO
text: Skipper, stand back. ’Tis age that nourisheth.

speaker: TRANIO
text: But youth in ladies’ eyes that flourisheth.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Content you, gentlemen. I will compound this strife.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: ’Tis deeds must win the prize, and he of both

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: That can assure my daughter greatest dower

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Shall have my Bianca’s love.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Say, Signior Gremio, what can you assure her?

speaker: GREMIO
text: First, as you know, my house within the city

speaker: GREMIO
text: Is richly furnishèd with plate and gold,

speaker: GREMIO
text: Basins and ewers to lave her dainty hands;

speaker: GREMIO
text: My hangings all of Tyrian tapestry;

speaker: GREMIO
text: In ivory coffers I have stuffed my crowns,

speaker: GREMIO
text: In cypress chests my arras counterpoints,

speaker: GREMIO
text: Costly apparel, tents, and canopies,

speaker: GREMIO
text: Fine linen, Turkey cushions bossed with pearl,

speaker: GREMIO
text: Valance of Venice gold in needlework,

speaker: GREMIO
text: Pewter and brass, and all things that belongs

speaker: GREMIO
text: To house or housekeeping. Then, at my farm

speaker: GREMIO
text: I have a hundred milch-kine to the pail,

speaker: GREMIO
text: Six score fat oxen standing in my stalls,

speaker: GREMIO
text: And all things answerable to this portion.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Myself am struck in years, I must confess,

speaker: GREMIO
text: And if I die tomorrow this is hers,

speaker: GREMIO
text: If whilst I live she will be only mine.

speaker: TRANIO
text:
That “only” came well in.
                 
                To
                 
                Baptista
                .
               Sir, list to me:

speaker: TRANIO
text: I am my father’s heir and only son.

speaker: TRANIO
text: If I may have your daughter to my wife,

speaker: TRANIO
text: I’ll leave her houses three or four as good,

speaker: TRANIO
text: Within rich Pisa walls, as any one

speaker: TRANIO
text: Old Signior Gremio has in Padua,

speaker: TRANIO
text: Besides two thousand ducats by the year

speaker: TRANIO
text: Of fruitful land, all which shall be her jointure.—

speaker: TRANIO
text: What, have I pinched you, Signior Gremio?

speaker: GREMIO
text: Two thousand ducats by the year of land?

speaker: GREMIO
text:
Aside
                .
               My land amounts not to so much in all.—

speaker: GREMIO
text: That she shall have, besides an argosy

speaker: GREMIO
text: That now is lying in Marcellus’ road.

speaker: GREMIO
text:
To
                 
                Tranio
                .
                 
              What, have I choked you with an argosy?

speaker: TRANIO
text: Gremio, ’tis known my father hath no less

speaker: TRANIO
text: Than three great argosies, besides two galliasses

speaker: TRANIO
text: And twelve tight galleys. These I will assure her,

speaker: TRANIO
text: And twice as much whate’er thou off’rest next.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Nay, I have offered all. I have no more,

speaker: GREMIO
text: And she can have no more than all I have.

speaker: GREMIO
text:
To
                 
                Baptista
                .
                 
              If you like me, she shall have me and mine.

speaker: TRANIO
text: Why, then, the maid is mine from all the world,

speaker: TRANIO
text: By your firm promise. Gremio is outvied.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: I must confess your offer is the best,

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: And, let your father make her the assurance,

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: She is your own; else, you must pardon me.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: If you should die before him, where’s her dower?

speaker: TRANIO
text: That’s but a cavil. He is old, I young.

speaker: GREMIO
text: And may not young men die as well as old?

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Well, gentlemen, I am thus resolved:

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: On Sunday next, you know

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: My daughter Katherine is to be married.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text:
To
                 
                Tranio
                 
                
                  as
                   
                  Lucentio
                  .
                   
                
              Now, on the Sunday following, shall Bianca

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: Be bride to you, if you make this assurance.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: If not, to Signior Gremio.

speaker: BAPTISTA
text: And so I take my leave, and thank you both.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Adieu, good neighbor.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Now I fear thee not.

speaker: GREMIO
text: Sirrah young gamester, your father were a fool

speaker: GREMIO
text: To give thee all and in his waning age

speaker: GREMIO
text: Set foot under thy table. Tut, a toy!

speaker: GREMIO
text: An old Italian fox is not so kind, my boy.

speaker: TRANIO
text: A vengeance on your crafty withered hide!—

speaker: TRANIO
text: Yet I have faced it with a card of ten.

speaker: TRANIO
text: ’Tis in my head to do my master good.

speaker: TRANIO
text: I see no reason but supposed Lucentio

speaker: TRANIO
text: Must get a father, called “supposed Vincentio”—

speaker: TRANIO
text: And that’s a wonder. Fathers commonly

speaker: TRANIO
text: Do get their children. But in this case of wooing,

speaker: TRANIO
text: A child shall get a sire, if I fail not of my cunning.
